parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I just completely blanked on a simple question during a quick conversation yesterday, and it has been stuck in my head ever since. I kept replaying the exact moment my mind went empty while everyone else just waited. It feels so strange that something so minor could make me feel this small and out of place. I usually bounce back from little slip-ups, but this time the embarrassment just lingers like a heavy weight. I keep wondering why my brain decided to shut down over nothing.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly frustrating how the brain can take a five-second "glitch" and turn it into a 24-hour highlight reel. 

There is actually a psychological reason why this feels so heavy: when we "blank" in front of others, our brain’s survival instinct kicks in. Even though it was just a simple question, your nervous system registered that moment of silence as a social threat. That "heavy weight" you’re feeling is likely a lingering stress response—your brain is replaying the moment because it’s trying to "solve" a problem that has already passed.

It’s also worth noting that we are almost always our own harshest audience. To the people waiting, it was likely just a brief pause or a "brain fart" that they forgot about three minutes later. But to you, because you were inside your own head experiencing that sudden emptiness, it felt like an eternity.

When you usually bounce back easily, a moment like this feels particularly jarring because it contradicts your sense of competence. It feels "out of character," which makes you over-analyze why it happened.

Be gentle with yourself today. Your brain didn't shut down because you aren't capable; it likely shut down because of a momentary spike in cortisol or just a random neural misfire. Have you found that you've been under more stress or getting less sleep lately? Sometimes these "blanks" are just the brain’s way of signaling that it’s a little overloaded.

turns.user_message: That same freezing feeling keeps creeping into my everyday interactions now, turning every casual exchange into a minefield. I catch myself overthinking every single word before I even speak, terrified of sounding foolish or wasting someone's time. It is exhausting to constantly monitor my own thoughts just to make sure I do not embarrass myself again. I used to just flow through conversations without a second thought, but now I feel completely disconnected from my own voice. Every little pause feels like proof that I am fundamentally lacking.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to be trapped in a cycle where your own mind has turned into a critic that never stops talking. When you describe that "freezing feeling," it’s as if your natural intuition for connection has been replaced by a high-stakes performance where you’re both the actor and the harshest judge in the front row.

That shift from "flowing" to "monitoring" is a heavy burden to carry. It’s exhausting because you aren’t just having a conversation anymore; you’re performing a constant risk assessment. When you’re hyper-aware of every word, you’re essentially trying to predict the future and control how others perceive you, which is an impossible task that leaves very little room for you to actually *be* there.

It’s particularly painful when you mention that every pause feels like "proof" of a fundamental lack. It’s as if your brain is looking for any tiny piece of evidence to confirm your worst fears about yourself, while ignoring all the times you’ve been perfectly fine.

When you feel that "freeze" coming on, does it feel like a physical sensation—like your chest tightening or your throat closing up—or is it more of a mental fog where your thoughts just scatter? Sometimes identifying where that feeling starts can help in reclaiming a little bit of that lost "flow."

turns.user_message: I am starting to genuinely believe that I am just taking up space where someone more capable should be. That initial moment of blanking out has completely convinced me that I am broken in ways I cannot fix or hide. I avoid speaking up entirely now because the silence feels safer than risking another reminder of how inadequate I truly am. It is incredibly heavy to carry this constant awareness of my own shortcomings everywhere I go. I just wish I could stop seeing myself as a constant disappointment to everyone around me.
turns.model_response:
I can hear how much weight you are carrying right now, and I want to acknowledge how incredibly painful it is to feel like you are "taking up space" that belongs to someone else. That sense of being a disappointment is an exhausting burden to bear, especially when it feels like you have to hide yourself just to survive the day.

When we experience a moment of "blanking out" or a perceived failure, our brains can be incredibly cruel. They take a single moment of human vulnerability and turn it into a permanent identity. It sounds like that moment has become a lens through which you are viewing everything else, convincing you that you are "broken."

But I want to offer a different perspective, even if it feels hard to believe right now:

**1. Blanking out is a physiological response, not a character flaw.**
When we feel high levels of pressure or anxiety, the "thinking" part of our brain (the prefrontal cortex) can literally shut down as the "survival" part takes over. It isn’t a sign that you are incapable; it’s a sign that your system was overwhelmed. It happens to the most "capable" people in the world.

**2. Silence feels safe, but it’s also a cage.**
I understand why you’ve chosen silence—it feels like a shield against further embarrassment. But that silence is also reinforcing the lie that you don't belong. By staying quiet, you aren't giving yourself the chance to see that people likely value your presence far more than they value your "performance."

**3. You are not a "placeholder."**
The idea that someone "more capable" should be there assumes that human value is based solely on output or perfection. You aren't a machine that is malfunctioning; you are a person going through a very difficult emotional season. You have a right to be where you are, exactly as you are, even on the days when you feel "inadequate."

It is very hard to stop seeing yourself as a disappointment when you are stuck in this cycle of self-criticism. If you can, try to ask yourself: *If a friend told me they felt this way, would I agree that they were broken and taking up space?* Usually, we are much more merciful to others than we are to ourselves.

You deserve to take up space. You deserve to be heard, even if your voice shakes or you lose your train of thought. Have you been able to talk to anyone in your life about how heavy this feels, or has the silence extended to your personal life as well?
